coredata.dc:title: Built-in electric field-assisted charge separation over carbon dots-modified Bi2WO6 nanoplates for photodegradation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Qing
Liu, Lijun
Li, Shuangzhi
Liu, Rong
coredata.dc:description: Bi2WO6 photocatalyst has attracted much interest but suffers from easy recombination of electron-hole pairs. Herein carbon dots (CDs) as a cocatalyst were hydrothermally hybridized with Bi2WO6 nanoplates with aim of efficient separation of charge carriers. Electron transfer from CDs to Bi2WO6 across their interfaces upon contact was revealed by XPS results and further supported by differential charge density analyses. Such electron transfer led to a built-in electric field pointing from CDs to Bi2WO6, which drove photoinduced electrons flowing to CDs under light irradiation. DFT simulation showed the introduced electron was mainly distributed on CDs instead of Bi2WO6, which implied that CDs had impressive capability of taking photoinduced electrons under irradiation. Such efficient charge separation was experimentally supported by photoelectrochemical analyses. The superoxide radicals and holes were found to participate in the photodegradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/Bi2WO6 could be ascribed to the efficient charge separation and large specific area of Bi2WO6 nanoplates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Bismuth tungstate
Charge separation
DFT calculation
Carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. Inset: HRTEM image and the particle size distribution of CDs. (b) UV–vis and PL spectra of CDs. Inset: Photographs of CDs solution under visible light (left) and UV light (right).
(a) XRD patterns of Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6. (b) EDX spectrum of CDs/Bi2WO6.
(a, b) SEM images, (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of the resulting CDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates.
(a) N2 sorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size distributions and (b) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CDs/Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6.
(a) C 1s and (b) O 1s XPS spectra of CDs/Bi2WO6. (c) Bi 4f and (d) W 4f XPS spectra of CDs/Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6.

The electrostatic potentials along the normal direction of (a) Bi2WO6 (0 0 1) facets and (b) CDs (0 0 2) facets. The red dashed line represents Fermi level and the blue one represents vacuum energy level. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Optimized structure model and (b) differential charge density image of CDs/Bi2WO6. (c) Differential charge density image of CDs/Bi2WO6 after introducing one extra electron into the model. The isosurface value is 0.6 meV Å−3. Cyan and yellow represent electron depletion and accumulation, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) EIS Nyquist curves and (b) transient photocurrent responses of Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6 electrodes under visible light irradiation.

(a) Successive UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB solution in the presence of CDs/Bi2WO6 under visible light irradiation. (b) Plots of C t/C 0 versus reaction time for the photodegradation of RhB over CDs/Bi2WO6 with various loadings of CDs. (c) Plots of C t/C 0 versus reaction time and (d) the corresponding degradation rates for the photodegradation by adding various trapping agents.
Mott-Schottky plots of Bi2WO6 nanoplates recorded at various frequencies.
The proposed photocatalytic mechanism for the photodegradation over CDs/Bi2WO6, featuring the built-in electric field-assisted charge separation.

head.ce:title: Built-in electric field-assisted charge separation over carbon dots-modified Bi2WO6 nanoplates for photodegradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bi2WO6 photocatalyst has attracted much interest but suffers from easy recombination of electron-hole pairs. Herein carbon dots (CDs) as a cocatalyst were hydrothermally hybridized with Bi2WO6 nanoplates with aim of efficient separation of charge carriers. Electron transfer from CDs to Bi2WO6 across their interfaces upon contact was revealed by XPS results and further supported by differential charge density analyses. Such electron transfer led to a built-in electric field pointing from CDs to Bi2WO6, which drove photoinduced electrons flowing to CDs under light irradiation. DFT simulation showed the introduced electron was mainly distributed on CDs instead of Bi2WO6, which implied that CDs had impressive capability of taking photoinduced electrons under irradiation. Such efficient charge separation was experimentally supported by photoelectrochemical analyses. The superoxide radicals and holes were found to participate in the photodegradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/Bi2WO6 could be ascribed to the efficient charge separation and large specific area of Bi2WO6 nanoplates.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyeing in textile industry pours waste water containing organic compounds. Photocatalytic degradation with solar energy provides an attractive solution to this issue [1]. Bi2WO6 is one of the simplest Aurivillius oxides and consists of alternate stacking of [Bi2O2] and [WO6] octahedral layers with O atoms connecting each layer. Such layer structures together with chemical stability, nontoxicity and narrow band gap (∼2.70 eV) render Bi2WO6 an attractive candidate for photodegradation [2–6]. Like other monocomponent photocatalysts, however, Bi2WO6 still suffers from the rapid recombination of charge carriers [7,8].

Various strategies were adopted to promote the charge separation, including surface modification [9–14] and coupling with other semiconductors or cocatalysts [15–21]. Among them, coupling with an appropriate electron-cocatalyst can efficiently separate photoinduced electron from semiconductor to the cocatalyst [22,23], which could retard the recombination of charge carriers and meanwhile offer active sites for the subsequent photooxidation [24–26]. Noble metals are the most efficient cocatalyst due to their intrinsic electronic structures [27–33], but their practical applications are still limited by the scarcity and high cost. Therefore, developing earth-abundant alternative cocatalysts to meet the increasing demands of photocatalytic oxidation process is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much interest in photocatalysis community owing to their low cost, fast charge transfer, and electron reservoir ability [34–37]. CDs have extensively been employed in combining with semiconductors to promote photocatalytic performance by trapping and separating photoexcited electrons [38–41]. They functioned as an effective electron-cocatalyst and even became the potential alternative to noble-metal cocatalysts. Recently Bi2WO6 was physically mixed with CDs for photodegradation of VOCs [42,43]. But this kind of photocatalyst cannot be used for solution-phase reactions because the physical mixture would be detached in solution. Furthermore, the contact interfaces of this hybrid are usually insufficient and incompact because the loose contact cannot create perfect interfaces for efficient charge separation, which severely deteriorates the photocatalytic performance.

Herein, CDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates were hydrothermally synthesized by using CDs as the in-situ growth centers of Bi2WO6 nanoplates. Electrons in CDs were found to flow into Bi2WO6 upon hybridization as revealed by XPS and DFT simulation. Such electron transfer created a built-in electric field, which separated photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Such efficient charge separation accounted for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CDs/Bi2WO6. Our work provides experimental and theoretic insights into the charge separation of CDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates, which could motivate rational design of other carbon-involved photocatalysts.

CDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates were hydrothermally synthesized using carbon dots as heterogeneous nucleation and growth centers. The detailed synthesis and characterization can be found in Supplementary Material. The resulting CDs showed diameters ranging from 3 to 5 nm (Fig. 1 a), consistent with the size distribution obtained from the DLS technique (inset of Fig. 1a). The carbon dot showed clear lattice fringes of graphite carbon with d-spacing of 0.332 nm as revealed by HRTEM (inset of Fig. 1a). The CDs solution was light yellow under visible light but exhibited a bright blue 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 1, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 color under UV light of 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1b). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs solution reveled a strong band at 331 nm (Fig. 1b), corresponding to the n-π* transition of CO groups of CDs [44]. The PL spectra of the CDs solution revealed an optimal excitation wavelength at 347 nm and emission wavelength at 433 nm, which fitted with the optical properties of previous graphite carbon dots [37].

Fig. 2 a showed the XRD patterns of Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6 synthesized via the typical hydrothermal synthesis. Both Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6 revealed similar diffraction patterns corresponding to the tetragonal Bi2WO6 (JCPDF # 39-0256). No diffraction reflections belonging to CDs were found due to their low content in CDs/Bi2WO6. The EDX spectrum of CDs/Bi2WO6 (Fig. 2b) revealed the presence of C element along with W, Bi and O, indicating the successful hybridization of CDs with Bi2WO6. The actual loading of CDs was estimated to be 5.1 wt% according to the Bi2WO6 content from ICP-OES analysis, which is close to the value (5.3 wt%) estimated from the thermogravimetric (TG) result (Fig. S1).

The CDs/Bi2WO6 nanohybrid exhibited a shape of nanoplate with irregular edges and rough surfaces (Fig. 3 a). The width of the nanoplates was up to hundreds of nanometers. The thickness was ∼10 nm as estimated from the perpendicularly-standing nanoplates (Fig. 3b). The nanoplate morphology of CDs/Bi2WO6 was further proved by the TEM image of Fig. 3c. Note that the CDs cannot be distinguished from the low-resolution TEM image because of the negligible contrast of the tinny CDs as compared with Bi2WO6. Fig. 3d showed the HRTEM image of an individual CDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplate. It exhibited three sets of lattice fringes with d-spacings of 0.314, 0.272 and 0.332 nm, which corresponded to the (0 0 2) and (1 3 1) facets of Bi2WO6 and (0 0 2) facets of graphite carbon dots, respectively. Clearly, the Bi2WO6 nanoplate was polycrystalline. The loose aggregation of Bi2WO6 grains created nanopores within the nanoplates, consistent with the N2-sorption results discussed below. Moreover, Bi2WO6 grains and CDs closely contacted with each other and formed intimate interfaces, implying successful formation of heterostructures instead of their physical mixture. Such combination favored the interfacial charge transfer and the electron-hole separation.

The surface areas (S BET) and pore size distributions of Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6 were analyzed with nitrogen sorption isotherms. Each sample revealed the IV-type isotherm based on IUPAC classification and H3-type hysteresis loop in the P/P 0 of 0.5–0.95 (Fig. 4 a), suggesting the presence of nanopores. The S BET of CDs/Bi2WO6 nanohybrid (42.2 m2/g) was higher than that of Bi2WO6 (28.1 m2/g) owing to the hybridization of CDs. The pore size distributions were a little board (inset of Fig. 4a). The nanopores with 2–4 nm in sizes corresponded to the cavity from grain stacking within Bi2WO6 nanoplates. The large nanopores (>4 nm) corresponded to the random stacking of the nanoplates. Such nanopores provided abundant pathways for reactant transport and could promote the photocatalytic activity [45]. Fig. 4b showed the UV–vis DRS of Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6. Clearly, Bi2WO6 nanoplates exhibited an enhanced light absorption upon hybridization with CDs because of the CDs sensitization or the interfacial interaction between CDs and Bi2WO6.

XPS was used to explore the chemical states and interfacial interaction of CDs/Bi2WO6. The survey spectra (Fig. S2) showed the existence of Bi, W, O and C in the nanohybrid. CDs exhibited a dominant graphite-like sp2 carbon and a minority of oxygen-containing groups (Fig. 5 a) [46]. The asymmetric O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5b) of CDs/Bi2WO6 can be fitted with lattice oxygen (529.8 eV) of Bi2WO6 and surface hydroxyl (531.9 eV) [47]. To gain insights into the possible interfacial electron transfer, we carefully compared the Bi 4f and W 4f XPS spectra of pristine Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6 [48]. The Bi 4f XPS spectrum (Fig. 5c) of each sample revealed two symmetric signals corresponding to trivalent Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2. It was found that the BE of Bi 4f7/2 for CDs/Bi2WO6 showed a negative shift of 0.2 eV as compared with that for pristine Bi2WO6. A similar negative BE shift was also observed in the W 4f XPS spectra of Bi2WO6 and CDs/Bi2WO6 as shown in Fig. 5d. The apparent BE shifts proved that some electrons immigrated from CDs to Bi2WO6 upon hybridization [49–51].

DFT calculation was performed to explore the interfacial electron transfer between CDs and Bi2WO6. Work function (ϕ) was widely used to predict the electron transfer at the interfaces of nanohybrids. Fig. 6 showed the calculated electrostatic potentials along the normal direction of Bi2WO6 (0 0 1) and CDs (0 0 2) facets. The work functions of Bi2WO6 (0 0 1) and CDs (0 0 2) were estimated to be 5.86 eV and 4.23 eV according to the formula ϕ = E V − E F, where E V and E F represent the vacuum and Fermi energy levels, respectively. The higher ϕ of Bi2WO6 suggested that some electrons would transfer from CDs to Bi2WO6 upon their hybridization [52,53].

The calculated differential charge density reaffirmed the interfacial electron transfer as revealed in Fig. 7 (a–b). The electron depletion and accumulation are labelled in cyan and yellow, respectively. Clearly, some electrons in CDs were delocalized and flowed into Bi2WO6, fitting well with the XPS analyses. The electron transfer created an internal electric field across the interfaces pointing from CDs to Bi2WO6, greatly separating the photoinduced electron-hole pairs and enhancing the photocatalytic activity of CDs/Bi2WO6 [54].

To further simulate the charge separation of CDs/Bi2WO6 under light irradiation, we have introduced one extra electron into the model and investigated the distribution of the extra electron. Fig. 7c presented the differential charge density image of the model, which was defined as Δρ = ρ 2 − ρ 1, where ρ 2 and ρ 1 represented the total electron density of CDs/Bi2WO6 with and without the extra electron, respectively. Interestingly, the introduced electron was predominately distributed in CDs since the electron accumulation region in CDs was much larger than that of Bi2WO6. Further Bader charge analysis showed that CDs gained 0.62e while Bi2WO6 only gained 0.38e. This simulation implied that CDs could effectively trap the photoinduced electrons and separate the electron-hole pairs.

Such efficient charge separation of CDs/Bi2WO6 was experimentally supported by the transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). CDs/Bi2WO6 exhibited a higher photocurrent density than Bi2WO6 as shown in Fig. 8 a, suggesting the CDs hybridization promoted the electron-hole separation [55–57]. The EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 8b) showed that CDs/Bi2WO6 had a smaller arc radius than Bi2WO6. The smaller radius of the semicircular arc revealed a faster transfer of charge carriers in CDs/Bi2WO6. These photoelectrochemical analyses reaffirmed the efficient charge separation in CDs/Bi2WO6, in good agreement with the DFT simulation.

The activity of CDs/Bi2WO6 was evaluated by photodegradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The photodegradation of RhB is imperceptible when CDs were used as the photocatalyst. Fig. 9 a showed the successive UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB solution in the presence of CDs/Bi2WO6. The absorbance of RhB solution dropped gradually as the irradiation went on. After 30 min of irradiation, the solution revealed negligible absorbance of RhB in the full-range wavelength was, suggesting complete minimization of RhB. It is found the photocatalytic activity of CDs/Bi2WO6 depended on its composition and exhibited the maximum value when the loading of CDs was 5 wt% (Fig. 9b). The degradation rate (DR) of RhB for CDs/Bi2WO6 was estimated to be 98.1% after 30 min of irradiation, much higher than that (55.3%) for pristine Bi2WO6. After five photocatalytic runs, CDs/Bi2WO6 still showed a high DR value of 96.2%, exhibiting good recyclability (Fig. S3). Active species trapping experiments were performed (Fig. 9(c–d)). CDs/Bi2WO6 showed no apparent loss of activity when IPA was added to trap ∙OH. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance was obviously limited upon addition of BQ or EDTA-2Na into the solution, confirming that
O2− and h+ were the major active species for the photodegradation.

Mott-Schottky (M-S) curves were recorded to explore the band structure of the sample. The positive slope of each M-S plot recorded at different frequencies indicated Bi2WO6 was a typical n-type semiconductor (Fig. 10 ). Its flat-band potential (E FB) was estimated to be −0.795 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl, pH = 7) or −0.58 eV (vs. NHE). E FB of an n-type semiconductor is close to its conduction band level (E CB) [58,59]. Thus, the conduction band and valence band (E VB) levels of Bi2WO6 are estimated to be −0.58 eV and 2.12 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Note that E CB of Bi2WO6 is more negative than E(O2/
O2−) (−0.046 eV vs. NHE), indicating the photoinduced electrons can reduce O2 into
O2− radical. But the photoinduced holes cannot oxidize H2O into
OH due to the lower E VB as compared to E(H2O/
OH) (2.24 eV vs. NHE) [52]. Therefore, h+ and
O2− are theoretically the active species, in good accordance with the analysis of above trapping experiments.

Combing experimental results and DFT simulations, we proposed a photocatalytic mechanism to elucidate the photocatalytic enhancement of CDs/Bi2WO6 (Fig. 11 ). This mechanism featured the promotion effect of the built-in electric field on electron-hole separation under light irradiation. As discussed in Section 2.2, electrons in CDs would flow to Bi2WO6 due to their work function difference upon the formation of CDs/Bi2WO6. Such electron transfer thus created a built-in electric field with direction pointing from CDs to Bi2WO6 at their interfaces. This internal electric field forced electrons to flow towards CDs but repelled holes to approach CDs. Therefore, the photoexcited electrons in the CB of Bi2WO6 would migrate to CDs, while the photogenerated holes accumulated in the VB of Bi2WO6, achieving perfect spatial separation of electron-hole pairs. As-separated electrons were then combined with dissolved oxygen to generate
O2− radical as discussed above. The
O2− radical together with holes oxidized RhB into CO2 and H2O, realizing the photodegradation of RhB.

In summary, CDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method for visible light photodegradation. Electrons in CDs were found to flow into Bi2WO6 upon their hybridization and created a built-in electric field pointing from CDs to Bi2WO6 at their interfaces. This internal electric field induced photoinduced electrons to flow to CDs but repelled holes from approaching CDs, spatially separating electron-hole pairs. Such efficient charge separation was further evidenced by electrochemical characterizations and DFT simulations. The
O2− and h+ were the active species responsible for the photodegradation. The promising activity of CDs/Bi2WO6 was attributed to the efficient charge separation and large SBET of CDs/Bi2WO6 nanoplates.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51201116, 51310105015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.09.168.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
